content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 9 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 206–207 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_009_page_001.jpg, DEU_009_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 149–150

---

1 Hear, O Yisra'el: you are crossing over the Yarden this day to go in to dispossess nations greater and mightier than you, cities great and fortified up to the heavens —

2 a people great and tall, [the] sons of the Anakim, whom you know and of whom you have heard: "Who can stand before the sons of Anak?"

3 And you shall know today that the LORD your God — he is the one crossing before you [as] a consuming fire; he will destroy them and he will subdue them before you, and you shall dispossess them and cause them to perish quickly, as the LORD has spoken to you.

4 Do not say in your heart when the LORD your God drives them out from before you, "Because of my righteousness the LORD brought me in to possess this land" — but because of the wickedness of these nations the LORD is driving them out from before you.

5 Not because of your righteousness and the uprightness of your heart are you going in to possess their land, but because of the wickedness of these nations the LORD your God is driving them out from before you, and in order to establish the word which the LORD swore to your fathers, to Avraham, to Yitschaq, and to Ya'aqov.

6 And you shall know that not because of your righteousness does the LORD your God give you this good land to possess it — for you are a stiff-necked people [am qesheh oref].

7 Remember, do not forget, how you provoked the LORD your God in the wilderness; from the day that you went out from the land of Egypt until your coming to this place you have been rebelling against the LORD.

8 And at Horev you provoked the LORD to anger, and the LORD was angry enough with you to destroy you.

9 When I went up the mountain to receive the tablets of stone, the tablets of the covenant which the LORD made with you, then I stayed on the mountain forty days and forty nights; bread I did not eat and water I did not drink.

10 And the LORD gave me the two tablets of stone written with the finger of God, and upon them [were] all the words which the LORD spoke with you on the mountain from the midst of the fire on the day of the assembly.

11 And it was at the end of forty days and forty nights that the LORD gave me the two tablets of stone, the tablets of the covenant.

12 And the LORD said to me, "Arise, go down quickly from here, for your people whom you brought out from Egypt has acted corruptly; they have turned quickly from the way which I commanded them; they have made themselves a cast image [egel massekhah]."

13 And the LORD said to me, saying, "I have seen this people, and behold, they are a stiff-necked people [am qesheh oref].

14 Let me alone, that I may destroy them and blot out their name from under the heavens, and I will make you into a nation mightier and more numerous than they."

15 And I turned and came down from the mountain, and the mountain was burning with fire, and the two tablets of the covenant were in my two hands.

16 And I saw and behold, you had sinned against the LORD your God; you had made for yourselves a cast calf [egel massekhah] — you had turned quickly from the way which the LORD commanded you.

17 And I took hold of the two tablets and threw them from my two hands, and I smashed them before your eyes.

18 And I fell down before the LORD as at the first — forty days and forty nights; bread I did not eat and water I did not drink — because of all your sin which you sinned by doing evil in the eyes of the LORD to provoke him.

19 For I was afraid of the anger and the wrath with which the LORD was angry against you enough to destroy you. But the LORD listened to me also this time.

20 And against Aharon the LORD was very angry enough to destroy him; and I prayed also for Aharon at that time.

21 And your sin which you made, the calf, I took and I burned it with fire and I crushed it, grinding [it] well, until it was reduced to dust, and I threw its dust into the stream that came down from the mountain.

22 And at Tav'erah and at Massah and at Qivrot-haTa'avah you were provoking the LORD.

23 And when the LORD sent you from Qadesh-Barnea, saying, "Go up and possess the land which I have given you" — you rebelled against the command of the LORD your God and you did not believe him and you did not hearken to his voice.

24 You have been rebellious against the LORD from the day I knew you.

25 And I fell down before the LORD the forty days and the forty nights which I lay prostrate, because the LORD had said to destroy you.

26 And I prayed to the LORD and said, "O Lord GOD [Adonai YHWH], do not destroy your people and your inheritance whom you redeemed in your greatness, whom you brought out from Egypt with a strong hand.

27 Remember your servants Avraham, Yitschaq, and Ya'aqov; do not turn to the stubbornness of this people and to their wickedness and to their sin,

28 lest the land from which you brought us say, 'Because the LORD was not able to bring them into the land which he promised them, and because of his hating them, he brought them out to slay them in the wilderness.'

29 And they are your people and your inheritance whom you brought out by your great power and by your outstretched arm."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**V.1 — "fortified up to the heavens":** Hebrew בְּצֻרֹת בַּשָּׁמַיִם — lit. "fortified in the heavens" — a hyperbolic idiom for massively walled cities. The LXX renders τειχήρεις εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν (walled into the sky). Preserved literally.

**V.2 — Anakim:** Hebrew עֲנָקִים — the giant clans of Canaan known from Numbers 13. The LXX renders Ενακιμ. "Sons of Anak" (בְּנֵי עֲנָקִים) is the literal construct, rendered as given.

**V.3 — "consuming fire":** Hebrew אֵשׁ אֹכְלָה — "fire [that] devours/consumes." Applied directly to YHWH as the one preceding Yisra'el into battle. The LXX renders πῦρ καταναλίσκον (a fire consuming). Identical imagery.

**V.6 — am qesheh oref:** Hebrew עַם-קְשֵׁה-עֹרֶף — "a people stiff of neck." The construct chain is tight: qesheh (hard/stiff) + oref (back of neck). The LXX renders λαὸς σκληροτράχηλος (a hard-necked people). This phrase recurs at v.13 — both occurrences preserved identically.

**V.12 — egel massekhah:** Hebrew עֵגֶל מַסֵּכָה — "a calf, a cast [image]." מַסֵּכָה (massekhah) is from נָסַךְ (to pour/cast metal). The LXX renders μόσχον χωνευτόν (a poured/cast calf). "Cast image" preserves the metallurgical process; "molten calf" is also traditional but less precise.

**V.18 — "also this time" / "as at the first":** The phrase כָּרִאשֹׁנָה (as at the first/as the first time) in v.18 refers back to the original forty days of v.9. The repetition of the fasting formula (no bread, no water, forty days, forty nights) in both vv.9 and 18 is a deliberate structural parallel. Both occurrences are rendered with identical English.

**V.21 — grinding to dust:** The sequence of destructive acts on the calf (burn, crush, grind, throw into stream) uses four distinct verbs in Hebrew, each preserved. The LC manuscript (p.207) shows this verse clearly. LXX Vat. p.149–150 confirms the four-verb sequence.

**V.22 — Tav'erah, Massah, Qivrot-haTa'avah:** Three place-names preserved in Hebrew transliteration. These refer to episodes in Numbers/Exodus: "burning" (Taberah, Num 11:3), "testing" (Massah, Exod 17:7), and "graves of craving" (Kibroth-hattaavah, Num 11:34). The LXX preserves the names in Greek transliteration: Ἐμπυρισμός, Πειρασμός, Μνήματα ἐπιθυμίας — but these are translated rather than transliterated. The MT Hebrew place-names are preserved in EMET.

**V.26 — Adonai YHWH:** The text reads אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה — the compound title. The prayer address distinguishes this from other uses of YHWH alone. Rendered "O Lord GOD" with small capitals for YHWH per convention.

**V.28 — "his hating them":** Hebrew מִשִּׂנְאָתוֹ אֹתָם — "from his hating of them" — Moshe puts feared words into the mouth of the Egyptians: that YHWH brought Yisra'el out because he hated them. This is a rhetorical argument (reductio ad absurdum). The LXX renders διὰ τὸ μισεῖν αὐτὸν αὐτούς (because of his hating them). The caustic irony is preserved literally.

— EMET
